conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions.

First, let me review the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To get started, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. I have two children - Alex who is 17 and Blake who is 18. They both live at home with me here in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can see you have two children - Alex (17) and Blake (18) - who both live at home with you in the UK.

To assess their eligibility, I need to know about their education or training status, as this is important for children aged 16 and over.

**Is Alex (your 17-year-old) currently in approved education or training?** (For example, studying at school, college, or in approved training courses)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is still in college studying for A-levels.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you. Alex being in college studying for A-levels would count as approved education.

Now, regarding Blake (your 18-year-old): **Is Blake currently in approved education or training?** (For example, studying at school, college, or in approved training courses)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't in education or training anymore. They finished their studies.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Just to clarify a couple more details:

**When did Blake finish their studies?** (I need to know approximately how long ago they left education or training)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They finished a few months ago - it's been more than 20 weeks now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. Let me ask one final question to complete my assessment:

**Are either Alex or Blake currently working 24 hours or more per week, or receiving any benefits in their own right** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work that much or receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

Let me provide you with my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, here's my assessment:

**For Alex (17):** ✅ **You ARE eligible for Child Benefit**
Alex is currently studying for A-levels at college, which counts as approved education. Since Alex is under 20 and remains in approved education, you continue to qualify for Child Benefit.

**For Blake (18):** ❌ **You are NOT eligible for Child Benefit**
Blake finished their studies more than 20 weeks ago and is not currently in approved education or training. The eligibility for Child Benefit ends when a young person aged 16 or over leaves education or training, with only a 20-week grace period available (and that applies primarily to 16-17 year olds who register with careers services or the armed services). Since Blake is 18 and has been out of education for more than 20 weeks, Child Benefit is no longer available for them.

**What this means for you:**
You can continue to claim Child Benefit for Alex, but you should inform HMRC that Blake is no longer eligible so they can adjust your payments accordingly.

If you have any questions about this assessment, please feel free to ask!

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and is currently studying for A-levels at college, which counts as approved education. According to the eligibility rules, you normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. Alex lives at home with you in the UK, and is not working 24 hours or more per week or receiving benefits in their own right. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 18 years old and finished their education more than 20 weeks ago. According to the eligibility rules, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. However, Blake is 18 and has been out of education for more than 20 weeks. Blake is not currently in approved education or training, not working 24 hours or more per week, and not receiving benefits in their own right. As Blake is no longer in approved education or training and the 20-week grace period has expired, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children living at home in the UK. Alex (17) remains eligible as they are under 20 and in approved education (studying A-levels at college). Blake (18) is not eligible because they left education more than 20 weeks ago and are not currently in approved education or training. Child Benefit eligibility for children aged 16 and over requires them to be in approved education or training (if under 20), or within the 20-week grace period after leaving education (for 16-17 year olds only).

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old and is currently studying for A-levels at college, which counts as approved education. According to the eligibility rules, you normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. Alex lives at home with you in the UK, and is not working 24 hours or more per week or receiving benefits in their own right. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 18 years old and finished their education more than 20 weeks ago. According to the eligibility rules, you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services. However, Blake is 18 and has been out of education for more than 20 weeks. Blake is not currently in approved education or training, not working 24 hours or more per week, and not receiving benefits in their own right. As Blake is no longer in approved education or training and the 20-week grace period has expired, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children living at home in the UK. Alex (17) remains eligible as they are under 20 and in approved education (studying A-levels at college). Blake (18) is not eligible because they left education more than 20 weeks ago and are not currently in approved education or training. Child Benefit eligibility for children aged 16 and over requires them to be in approved education or training (if under 20), or within the 20-week grace period after leaving education (for 16-17 year olds only).
